text: Abraham took another wife, named Keturah.
She bore him Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.
Jokshan fathered Sheba and Dedan, whose descendants were the Asshurim, Letushim, and Leummim.
Midian's sons were Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah — all descendants of Keturah.
Abraham gave everything he owned to Isaac.
To the sons of his concubines he gave gifts and sent them away east, away from Isaac, while he still lived.
Abraham lived a hundred seventy-five years.
Abraham breathed his last and died in a good old age, old and full of years, and was gathered to his people.
His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him in the cave of Machpelah, in Ephron's field near Mamre.
It was the field Abraham had bought from the Hittites, where Abraham and his wife Sarah were both buried.
After Abraham's death, God blessed Isaac, who lived near Beer-lahai-roi.
This is the account of Ishmael, Abraham's son by Hagar the Egyptian, Sarah's servant.
These are Ishmael's sons by name in birth order: Nebaioth, then Kedar, Adbeel, and Mibsam.
Mishma, Dumah, and Massa.
Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.
These were Ishmael's twelve sons by name, according to their villages and camps, twelve tribal princes.
Ishmael lived a hundred thirty-seven years, then breathed his last and was gathered to his people.
His descendants settled from Havilah to Shur near Egypt, toward Assyria, living in hostility toward all their relatives.
This is the account of Isaac, Abraham's son.
Isaac was forty when he married Rebekah, daughter of Bethuel the Aramean of Paddan-aram, sister of Laban.
Isaac prayed to the LORD for his wife, since she was childless, and the LORD granted his prayer, and Rebekah conceived.
The children struggled together inside her, and she asked why this was happening to her, and went to inquire of the LORD.
The LORD told her two nations were in her womb, two peoples would be divided, one stronger than the other, and the older would serve the younger.
When her time came, there were indeed twins in her womb.
The first came out red, covered with hair like a cloak, and they named him Esau.
Then his brother came out gripping Esau's heel, so he was named Jacob; Isaac was sixty when they were born.
The boys grew up; Esau became a skillful hunter, a man of the field, while Jacob was quiet, staying among the tents.
Isaac loved Esau because he had a taste for wild game, but Rebekah loved Jacob.
Once when Jacob was cooking stew, Esau came in from the field exhausted.
Esau asked Jacob to let him gulp down some of the red stew, for he was famished — that is why he was named Edom.
Jacob said he would give it only if Esau sold him his birthright first.
Esau said he was about to die, so what good was a birthright to him.
Jacob made him swear to it first, and Esau swore, selling his birthright to Jacob.
Jacob gave him bread and lentil stew; he ate, drank, got up, and left — Esau despised his birthright.
